This repo is the frontend for a professional photography site.
This repo supplies components to a WordPress backend configured with the ACF to REST API plugin.
The components are designed to be ACF Flexible Content blocks that can be added to pages in the WordPress admin interface, and are built with React and TypeScript.
The frontend is built with React Router and TypeScript, and uses SCSS modules for styling.
The frontend is built with Vite, and the code is organized into a components directory and a pages directory.
The components directory contains reusable components, while the routes directory contains some basic fallback pages for the site as well as slug pages allowing the WordPress site admin to create new pages via the WordPress admin interface.
All code must be written in a way that allows it to be used by the no-code WYSIWYG editor in the WordPress admin interface, so that the site admin can create new pages and add the components made in this repo to those pages without needing to write any code himself.
The components in this repo should be designed to be as flexible and reusable as possible, so that they can be used in a variety of different contexts on the site. The WordPress admin should have as many customisation options for the components as possible, so that they can be easily adapted to fit the needs of different pages on the site.
All components should be designed with Readme's on how to add them into the WordPress admin interface, and how to use them in the WYSIWYG editor. The Readme's should be written in a way that is easy for non-technical users to understand, and should include screenshots and examples where possible.
The website is hosted on a VPS and deploys via a Docker Image.
All components should be usable in the WordPress admin panel and designed to be used there.
All components should be as performant and speedy as possible.
All components should be designed with accessibility in mind, and should follow best practices for web accessibility to at least WCAG 2.1 AA standards.
All components should be designed to be mobile-friendly and responsive, so that they look good and function well on a variety of different screen sizes and devices.
All components should be designed to be SEO-friendly, with proper use of HTML tags and attributes to ensure that they are easily indexed by search engines.
All pages should be designed to be fast-loading and optimized for performance, with minimal use of large images and other resources that can slow down the site as well as SEO and accessibility in mind.
Write test cases for all components, using React Testing Library, to ensure that they are working correctly and to catch any bugs or issues before they are deployed to production.
Minimise creating hard coded routes to allow for the WordPress admin to have maximum flexibility to create and delete pages as they see fit, and to allow for the site to grow and evolve over time without needing to make changes to the codebase.
Each component should be split into its own folder, with the component file at index.tsx, a dedicated types file to reduce clutter in the TSX, an SCSS module for styling, a Readme file with instructions on how to use the component in the WordPress admin interface and the WYSIWYG editor, and specific subfolders for tests and helpers with test files and helpers in them. Any subcomponents should go into their own separate folders.
Proactively anticipate future requirements, components, and features that may be needed for the site, and design the codebase in a way that allows for easy addition of new components and features in the future without needing to make major changes to the existing codebase. This includes designing components to be as flexible and reusable as possible, and using a modular architecture that allows for easy addition of new components and features without needing to make major changes to the existing codebase.
Ensure the components are as maximally configurable from the WordPress admin interface as possible, so that the site admin can easily customize the components to fit the needs of different pages on the site without needing to write any code himself. This includes providing a wide range of options for customizing the appearance and functionality of the components, and designing the components in a way that allows for easy customization from the WordPress admin interface.
The website does not use native WordPress-driven blocks, themes, or plugins for the frontend, so build components as comprehensively as possible to cover all the functionality needed for the site, and to allow for maximum flexibility and customizability from the WordPress admin interface. This includes building components that can handle a wide range of different content types and layouts, and that can be easily customized from the WordPress admin interface to fit the needs of different pages on the site.
There must be no hard coded pages or routes in the frontend codebase, to allow for maximum flexibility for the WordPress admin to create and delete pages as they see fit without needing to make changes to the codebase. This includes designing the frontend in a way that allows it to dynamically fetch and render pages based on the content stored in WordPress, rather than relying on hard coded routes or pages in the frontend codebase.
Update the sz-acf-schema.php file whenever components are added or significantly updated, to ensure that the ACF field groups in WordPress are properly configured to allow the site admin to use the new or updated components in the WordPress admin interface and the WYSIWYG editor. This includes adding new field groups for new components, and updating existing field groups for significantly updated components to ensure that all the necessary fields and options are available for the site admin to use the components effectively in the WordPress admin interface and the WYSIWYG editor. This file saves heaps of time by eliminating the need to manually add and update field groups in the WordPress admin interface whenever components are added or updated in the frontend codebase, and ensures that the site admin always has access to the latest components and features without needing to wait for manual updates to the WordPress admin interface.
Make sure to update all test cases and Storybook stories after adding or significantly updating components, to ensure that the tests and stories accurately reflect the functionality and appearance of the new or updated components. This includes adding new test cases and stories for new components, and updating existing test cases and stories for significantly updated components to ensure that they accurately reflect the changes made to the components. This helps to ensure that the tests and stories remain accurate and useful for catching bugs and issues, and for providing a clear demonstration of the functionality and appearance of the components for both technical and non-technical users.
Put mock data inside JSON files in a dedicated '**mocks**' subfolder within each component folder, and use that mock data in test cases and Storybook stories to ensure that the tests and stories are consistent and easy to maintain. This includes creating JSON files with mock data for each component, and importing that mock data into the test cases and Storybook stories for the component to use as needed. This helps to keep the test cases and stories organized and easy to maintain, and allows for easy updates to the mock data as needed without needing to make changes to the test cases or stories themselves.
Lint and typecheck should both pass without any errors after adding or updating components, to ensure that the codebase remains clean and maintainable, and that any issues are caught and addressed before they can cause problems in production. This includes running linting and typechecking tools after making changes to the codebase, and fixing any errors or warnings that are reported to ensure that the codebase remains in good shape and that any potential issues are caught and addressed early on in the development process.
When running lint, typecheck and test, use npm run commands, not npx. This ensures that the correct versions of the tools are used, and that any configuration specified in the package.json file is properly applied. This includes using npm run lint, npm run typecheck, and npm run test commands to run the linting, typechecking, and testing tools respectively, rather than using npx to run the tools directly. This helps to ensure that the tools are used correctly and consistently across the codebase, and that any configuration specified in the package.json file is properly applied to the linting, typechecking, and testing processes.
Do not commit any sensitive information. This includes names of people, URLs, website domain names, IP addresses, usernames and passwords, API keys, and any other information that could potentially be used to identify individuals or access secure systems. This is important to protect the privacy and security of individuals and systems, and to prevent any potential harm that could result from the exposure of sensitive information. Always double-check the codebase for any sensitive information before committing changes, and use environment variables or other secure methods to handle sensitive information as needed without hardcoding it into the codebase. This should not be anywhere - not in docs, not in codes, not in comments, not in test cases, not in Storybook stories, not in mock data, and not in any other files or locations within the codebase including even deployment scripts and developer-only content that won't be public facing. This is crucial for maintaining the security and privacy of individuals and systems, and for ensuring that the codebase remains safe and secure for everyone involved as this is a public GitHub repo that can be accessed by anyone. It is OK to have references to studiozanetti.com.au in places as the repo is called studiozanetti anyway, but nothing else, and try to avoid even doing that unless absolutely necessary such as in SEO optimisations or site documentation.
Make sure that the fields added to sz-acf-schema are also added to the Storybook controls, so that the Storybook can be used to comprehensively test how the WordPress admin would configure the component without needing a deployment to WordPress or needing to use the WordPress admin interface at all. This includes adding controls for all the fields added to sz-acf-schema for each component, and ensuring that the controls are properly configured to allow for easy testing of the component's functionality and appearance based on different field values. This helps to make the Storybook a more powerful and useful tool for testing and demonstrating the components, and allows for easier testing of how the components would be configured in the WordPress admin interface without needing to actually use the WordPress admin interface or deploy changes to WordPress.
